---
title: "2.12 Informal Run-Time Analysis"
subtitle: "Part 3: POGIL Analyzing Loops"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Groupwork Coding Challenge

### POGIL Analyzing Loops

We encourage you to do this activity as a POGIL, Process Oriented Guided Inquiry Learning, group activity.

POGIL groups are self-managed teams of up to 4 students where everyone has a role and works together to solve the problems, making sure that everyone in the team participates and learns.

## Group Directions

### Trace tables and formulas

Do the following exercises in your group.

Make sure you draw the trace tables keeping track of all the variables in the loops.

Use the formulas to determine how many times the loops run.

## POGIL Question

### `mchoice:: qln1`

How many times does the following code print a `*`?

```java
for (int i = 3; i < 8; i++)
{
    for (int y = 1; y < 5; y++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `40`
- B. `20`
- C. `24`
- D. `30`
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: qln1`

Correct answer: <span class="mark">B. `20`</span>

The outer loop executes `7 - 3 + 1 = 5` times and the inner loop executes `4 - 1 + 1 = 4` times.

This will print `5 * 4 = 20` stars.

## POGIL Question

### `mchoice:: qln2`

What does the following code print?

```java
for (int i = 2; i < 8; i++)
{
    for (int y = 1; y <= 5; y++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. A rectangle of 8 rows with 5 stars per row.
- B. A rectangle of 8 rows with 4 stars per row.
- C. A rectangle of 6 rows with 5 stars per row.
- D. A rectangle of 6 rows with 4 stars per row.
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: qln2`

Correct answer: <span class="mark">C. A rectangle of 6 rows with 5 stars per row.</span>

The outer loop executes `8 - 2 + 1 = 6` times, so there are `6` rows.

The inner loop executes `5 - 1 + 1 = 5` times, so there are `5` columns.

## POGIL Question

### `mchoice:: qln3`

What does the following print?

```java
for (int i = 3; i <= 9; i++)
{
   for (int j = 6; j > 0; j--)
   {
       System.out.print("*");
   }
   System.out.println();
}
```

::: {.tight-list}
- A. A rectangle of 9 rows and 5 stars per row.
- B. A rectangle of 6 rows and 6 stars per row.
- C. A rectangle of 7 rows and 5 stars per row.
- D. A rectangle of 7 rows and 6 stars per row.
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: qln3`

Correct answer: <span class="mark">D. A rectangle of 7 rows and 6 stars per row.</span>

The outer loop executes `9 - 3 + 1 = 7` times.

The inner loop executes `6 - 1 + 1 = 6` times.

## POGIL Question

### `mchoice:: qln4`

Consider the following code segment.

How many times is the string `"Hi!"` printed as a result of executing the code segment?

```java
int i = 0;
while (i <= 4)
{
  for (int j = 0; j < 3; j++)
  {
    System.out.println("Hi!");
  }
  i++;
}
```

## POGIL Choices

### `mchoice:: qln4`

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `15`
- B. `12`
- C. `10`
- D. `8`
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### `mchoice:: qln4`

Correct answer: <span class="mark">A. `15`</span>

The outer loop executes `4 - 0 + 1 = 5` times.

The inner loop runs `3` times for `j = 0`, `1`, and `2`.

So `"Hi!"` is printed `5 * 3 = 15` times.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- draw trace tables before choosing an answer
- convert `<` loop bounds into the largest value that enters the body
- multiply rectangular nested-loop counts
- identify rows and columns in printed rectangle outputs
- handle decrementing inner loops by listing valid counter values
- combine a `while` outer loop with a `for` inner loop by counting both loops
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
